questions.prompt: Which description best matches DNA in this section?
choices: A protein chain that directly makes every visible trait without genetic instructions.
A chromosome photograph arranged by size and shape.
A single nitrogenous base that works without being part of a larger molecule.
A double-stranded nucleic acid that stores genetic information.
questions.explanation: DNA, or deoxyribonucleic acid, is genetic material that stores information.

questions.prompt: Why can DNA help explain inherited traits?
choices: DNA is copied from the environment after a trait is learned.
DNA matters only in reproductive cells and not in body cells.
Organisms receive genetic information in DNA from their biological parent or parents.
DNA stores only temporary instructions that disappear before development begins.
questions.explanation: Inherited traits are connected to genetic information passed from biological parents or a parent.

questions.prompt: Which statement corrects the misconception that DNA is only about appearance?
choices: DNA can influence appearance and life processes inside cells.
DNA controls inherited traits but has no connection to protein production.
DNA affects only traits that can be seen without a microscope.
DNA is important in animals but not in plants, bacteria, protists, or fungi.
questions.explanation: The textbook connects DNA to appearance and life processes, not just visible traits.

kp-2.title: KP2: DNA and variation among living things

questions.prompt: Two patches of grass grow differently in similar conditions. Which explanation best connects this variation to DNA?
choices: The patches must belong to different kingdoms because variation cannot occur within plants.
The difference must be only environmental because DNA affects reproduction but not structures.
Differences in DNA may influence traits such as root systems, stems, or leaf shape.
The patches must have identical DNA because they are both grass.
questions.explanation: The textbook uses plants as one example where DNA differences can contribute to structural variation.

questions.prompt: Why can two animal species share basic features but still look very different?
choices: They have the same DNA sequence but choose to use different body plans.
They share cell structures, so their DNA cannot affect their appearance.
Their differences are caused only by habitat, while inherited information plays no role.
They share some life characteristics, but DNA differences can influence body shape, limbs, and organ systems.
questions.explanation: Animals share broad characteristics, while DNA differences help produce variation among species.

questions.prompt: Which example best fits DNA-related variation among living things?
choices: Two organisms of the same species always having identical inherited traits.
Some bacteria are helpful decomposers while others can cause disease.
Different ecosystems containing different amounts of sunlight but no inherited variation.
A species changing because every individual learned exactly the same behaviour.
questions.explanation: Bacteria are living things, and differences in DNA can help them live in different ways and places.

questions.prompt: Which set lists characteristics shared by living things in this section?
choices: Being multicellular, photosynthetic, and able to move quickly.
Being made of cells, using energy, growing, and reproducing.
Having the same DNA sequence and the same body structures.
Having chromosomes visible at all times and never needing energy.
questions.explanation: The text identifies cells, energy use, growth, and reproduction as shared characteristics of life.

questions.prompt: A bacterium, a mushroom, and a crow are very different. What still connects them as living things?
choices: They all have the same organ systems and body plan.
They all photosynthesize to make their own food.
They carry out basic life processes and are linked to DNA.
They all belong to the same species because they are alive.
questions.explanation: Different organism groups can share basic life characteristics even when their structures differ.

questions.prompt: Which comparison best separates shared life traits from DNA-based variation?
choices: Organisms that reproduce must have identical DNA and identical traits.
DNA explains shared life processes, while cells explain all variation.
Variation among organisms means they cannot share any life characteristics.
All living grass uses energy and grows, but different grass plants can vary in leaf shape.
questions.explanation: Growth and energy use are shared life traits, while leaf-shape differences can reflect variation.

questions.prompt: Where is genetic information found in most cells of an organism?
choices: In proteins after they have already been assembled.
In DNA inside the cell.
Only in visible body parts, not in internal cells.
In the environment first, then copied into DNA after birth.
questions.explanation: Most cells contain genetic information, and that information can influence appearance and life processes.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows DNA influencing a life process rather than only a visible trait?
choices: DNA changes only the colour of an organism's outer surface.
A protein changes DNA into a chromosome only after the cell stops functioning.
DNA information affects a protein that helps a cell function.
A life process happens independently of genetic information in every cell.
questions.explanation: DNA information can affect proteins and cell functions, not just visible structures.

questions.prompt: A student says, 'DNA only explains why organisms look different.' What is the best correction?
choices: DNA affects body shape but cannot affect cell function.
DNA affects proteins only in bacteria, not in plants or animals.
DNA is useful for inheritance but not for development.
DNA can influence both appearance and internal life processes.
questions.explanation: The textbook links DNA to an organism's appearance and life processes.

kp-5.title: KP5: DNA across major groups of organisms

questions.prompt: Which group is included in the textbook examples of DNA-related variation?
choices: Only multicellular organisms with organs.
Only species that reproduce sexually.
Only organisms large enough to see without a microscope.
Plants, animals, bacteria, protists, and fungi.
questions.explanation: The section connects DNA-based variation to many organism groups, including microscopic groups.

questions.prompt: Which example best connects DNA to diversity across organism groups?
choices: Plants and bacteria vary only because of climate, while animals vary only because of DNA.
All organisms use DNA in exactly the same sequence, so diversity must come only from ecosystems.
Plants can vary in roots and leaves, animals in body structures, and bacteria in how they live.
Fungi and protists show diversity, but it is unrelated to inherited information.
questions.explanation: The textbook uses several groups to show that DNA is connected to the diversity of life.

questions.prompt: Why is it misleading to say that DNA matters only for large organisms?
choices: Small organisms have proteins instead of inherited information.
Only organisms with tissues can have genetic material.
DNA affects small organisms only when they become multicellular.
Microscopic organisms such as bacteria and protists also show DNA-related variation.
questions.explanation: The textbook includes bacteria and protists as examples of living things with DNA-related variation.

questions.prompt: Which two nucleic acids are named in this section?
choices: DNA and protein.
RNA and amino acid chains.
Chromatin and chromosome.
DNA and RNA.
questions.explanation: Cells contain nucleic acids such as DNA and RNA.

questions.prompt: What are nucleic acids made from?
choices: Only nitrogenous bases with no sugar or phosphate.
Whole chromosomes linked together end to end.
Smaller components called nucleotides.
Amino acids folded into protein chains.
questions.explanation: Nucleic acids are large molecules built from nucleotide units.

questions.prompt: Which statement best avoids a common mix-up?
choices: DNA and RNA are nucleic acids, while proteins are a different type of molecule.
DNA and RNA are proteins because both help cells make proteins.
Nucleic acids are chromosomes, so nucleotides are complete chromosome pairs.
RNA is a nucleic acid only after it has been translated into a protein.
questions.explanation: DNA and RNA are nucleic acids; proteins are discussed as products made from genetic instructions.

questions.prompt: What three parts make up a nucleotide?
choices: A chromosome, a gene, and an allele.
A phosphate group, a sugar, and a nitrogenous base.
A protein, an amino acid, and a folded chain.
A base pair, a chromosome pair, and a karyotype.
questions.explanation: Each nucleotide consists of a phosphate group, a sugar, and a nitrogenous base.

questions.prompt: In a DNA model, what does one nucleotide represent?
choices: One repeating building block in a DNA strand.
One complete double helix made from many chromosomes.
One protein instruction after RNA has already been translated.
One full karyotype arranged from longest to shortest chromosome.
questions.explanation: DNA strands are built from many nucleotide units linked together.

questions.prompt: Which student statement needs correction?
choices: A nucleotide has a sugar.
A nucleotide has a phosphate group.
A nucleotide has a nitrogenous base.
A nucleotide is made of two complete DNA molecules.
questions.explanation: A nucleotide is one building block, not two complete DNA molecules.

questions.prompt: Which list contains the four nitrogenous bases in DNA?
choices: Adenine, cytosine, guanine, and thymine.
Adenine, cytosine, guanine, and uracil.
Sugar, phosphate, chromatin, and chromosome.
Gene, allele, karyotype, and species.
questions.explanation: The four DNA bases are adenine (A), cytosine (C), guanine (G), and thymine (T).

questions.prompt: What do the letters \(A\), \(C\), \(G\), and \(T\) stand for in DNA?
choices: Four types of complete nucleotides, including sugar and phosphate.
Nitrogenous bases.
Four chromosome shapes used in a karyotype.
Four amino acids that make up the sugar-phosphate backbone.
questions.explanation: A, C, G, and T are shorthand for the four nitrogenous bases in DNA.

questions.prompt: Why are the bases important to genetic information?
choices: Their order in DNA helps store instructions used by cells.
They make the sides of the DNA ladder but do not affect the rungs.
They matter only because all DNA molecules have the same base order.
They replace genes, so chromosomes do not need DNA.
questions.explanation: The sequence of bases along DNA stores genetic information.

questions.prompt: Which model best describes DNA structure?
choices: Two nucleotide strands arranged like a twisted ladder.
One strand of amino acids folded into a protein.
A chromosome photograph sorted from longest to shortest.
A single row of bases with no sugar-phosphate backbone.
questions.explanation: DNA is described as a double helix, like a twisted ladder made of two nucleotide strands.

questions.prompt: In the twisted-ladder model of DNA, what forms the sides of the ladder?
choices: Pairs of nitrogenous bases joined in the centre.
Sugar and phosphate groups.
Genes arranged from longest to shortest.
RNA copies waiting to become proteins.
questions.explanation: The sugar-phosphate parts of nucleotides form the sides, or backbone, of the DNA ladder.

questions.prompt: What forms the rungs of the DNA ladder?
choices: Sugar-phosphate groups bonded along one strand.
Pairs of nitrogenous bases.
Homologous chromosome pairs lined up in a karyotype.
Protein chains made after RNA translation.
questions.explanation: Each rung is made from two nitrogenous bases bonded together as a base pair.

questions.prompt: Which base-pairing rule is correct for DNA?
choices: \(A\) pairs with \(G\), and \(T\) pairs with \(C\).
\(A\) pairs only with \(A\), and \(C\) pairs only with \(C\).
\(A\) pairs with \(T\), and \(G\) pairs with \(C\).
\(A\) pairs with \(C\), and \(G\) pairs with \(T\).
questions.explanation: Complementary bases pair in a specific way: A with T, and G with C.

questions.prompt: If one DNA strand has the sequence \(ATGGCTA\), what is the complementary strand?
choices: \(ATGGCTA\)
\(TACCGAT\)
\(TAGGCTA\)
\(UACCGUA\)
questions.explanation: Apply the pairing rules base by base: A-T, T-A, G-C, G-C, C-G, T-A, A-T.

questions.prompt: Why does complementary base pairing matter for DNA structure?
choices: It lets the two strands match in a specific way along the molecule.
It means the two DNA strands must have identical base sequences.
It explains why the sugar-phosphate backbone stores the base order by itself.
It allows any base to pair with any other base as long as the molecule twists.
questions.explanation: Specific base pairing helps the two DNA strands fit together and supports accurate information storage.

kp-11.title: KP6: DNA sequence and protein instructions

questions.prompt: What part of DNA stores instructions for cells?
choices: The total amount of sugar and phosphate in a chromosome.
The number of chromosomes in a nucleus, no matter how bases are arranged.
The order of A, T, C, and G bases along a DNA strand.
The shape of the nucleus around the chromosomes.
questions.explanation: Genetic information is connected to the sequence, or order, of bases in DNA.

questions.prompt: A cell uses a gene when it needs to build a certain protein. Which connection is most accurate?
choices: DNA is changed directly into a finished protein without any intermediate steps.
A gene's base sequence is used to make an RNA copy, which helps assemble amino acids in order.
A protein chooses which DNA sequence the cell will inherit after the protein is made.
Only the phosphate groups decide the amino acid sequence of a protein.
questions.explanation: The base sequence in DNA carries instructions that help determine which protein is made.

questions.prompt: Which statement corrects a common misconception about DNA instructions?
choices: Two DNA segments with the same numbers of each base must always carry the same message.
DNA instructions depend mainly on how tightly chromatin is coiled.
A shorter DNA sequence always stores simpler information than a longer one.
The same four bases can store different instructions when their order changes.
questions.explanation: DNA uses base sequences; changing the order can change the information carried.

questions.prompt: Why are proteins important to organism function?
choices: They help form cell and tissue structures and help control cell activities.
They are mainly containers that store chromosomes during interphase.
They replace DNA during cell division so chromosomes are not needed.
They determine traits without being connected to DNA instructions.
questions.explanation: Proteins are linked to both structures in organisms and many cell functions.

questions.prompt: A mutation changes instructions in a gene used by skin cells. Which result is most scientifically reasonable?
choices: The change would affect only chromosome size, not any molecule made from the gene.
Every protein in the organism would become identical to that gene.
The protein made from that information might change, affecting how those cells work.
The mutation would remove the whole genome from cells that use the gene.
questions.explanation: DNA instructions influence proteins, and proteins can affect how cells form and function.

questions.prompt: Which misconception about proteins is best corrected?
choices: Proteins only matter in muscles, while other cells do not use them.
Proteins do more than build muscles; many proteins help control cell processes.
Proteins are inherited unchanged from parents instead of being made using instructions.
Proteins are the same as genes because both are sections of DNA.
questions.explanation: Proteins have many roles, including structural roles and control of cell activities.

questions.prompt: What is an organism's genome?
choices: One gene that controls the organism's most visible trait.
All of the proteins a cell happens to make during one day.
Only the chromosomes visible in a karyotype photo.
The complete DNA sequence in the cells of that organism.
questions.explanation: A genome refers to the complete DNA sequence of an organism.

questions.prompt: Which statement best connects the human genome to chromosomes?
choices: The genome is distributed across the chromosomes in human body cells.
The genome is stored only in blood plasma, while chromosomes are empty supports.
Each cell keeps only one gene from the genome and discards the rest.
Only chromosome 1 carries DNA; the other chromosomes contain only protein.
questions.explanation: In humans, DNA is organized into chromosomes rather than existing as one loose sequence.

questions.prompt: A student says a genome is just one gene. What is the best correction?
choices: A genome is the protein made from one gene.
A genome is one chromosome pair selected from a karyotype.
A genome includes all of an organism's DNA sequence, not just one gene.
A genome is a base pair that appears only in bacteria.
questions.explanation: A gene is one section of DNA, while a genome refers to the complete DNA sequence.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best describes how long DNA is organized in a dividing cell?
choices: Chromosomes are broken into loose bases, which then become chromatin.
DNA is packaged as chromatin, which can condense into chromosomes.
Genes turn into amino acids first, and the amino acids coil into chromosomes.
DNA leaves the nucleus and becomes part of the cell membrane.
questions.explanation: DNA is associated with chromatin, and chromatin can condense into visible chromosomes during cell division.

questions.prompt: Why is packaging DNA into chromatin and chromosomes useful?
choices: It changes the DNA code into RNA before every cell division.
It removes base sequences that are not needed in body cells.
It changes genes into alleles by rearranging every chromosome.
It helps a very long DNA molecule fit and organize inside a small nucleus.
questions.explanation: DNA must be compactly organized to fit in the nucleus and to be handled during division.

questions.prompt: Which statement avoids a common mix-up?
choices: Chromosomes are condensed structures made of DNA-containing chromatin.
Chromosomes and DNA are separate materials that never occur together.
Chromatin is the same thing as a single nucleotide base.
Chromosomes are proteins that carry DNA outside the nucleus.
questions.explanation: Chromosomes are an organized, condensed form of DNA-containing material.

kp-15.title: KP2: Chromosome number in human body cells

questions.prompt: What is the usual chromosome arrangement in a human somatic cell?
choices: 23 chromosomes arranged as 46 pairs.
46 pairs, because each chromosome pair is counted as two pairs.
46 chromosomes arranged as 23 pairs.
22 chromosomes plus the X and Y chromosomes in every person.
questions.explanation: Human body cells usually contain 46 chromosomes organized into 23 pairs.

questions.prompt: A microscope image of a typical human body cell shows chromosome pairs. What should you expect for the total number?
choices: 23 chromosomes total, because each pair is counted as one chromosome.
46 chromosomes total.
46 pairs, for a total of 92 chromosomes.
24 chromosomes total, because sex chromosomes are separate from autosomes.
questions.explanation: A typical human somatic cell has 46 chromosomes, arranged as pairs.

questions.prompt: Which correction is most accurate?
choices: A chromosome pair means four identical chromosomes copied from one parent.
Human body cells normally have 23 chromosomes, not 23 pairs.
Chromosome number can be determined only by reading every DNA base.
The number of chromosome pairs is not the same as the total number of chromosomes.
questions.explanation: In humans, 23 pairs means 46 chromosomes total, because each pair contains two chromosomes.

questions.prompt: Which description correctly separates autosomes from sex chromosomes in humans?
choices: All chromosome pairs are autosomes unless a cell is involved in reproduction.
Most pairs are autosomes, and one pair is the sex chromosomes.
Autosomes are chromosome copies made after DNA replication.
Sex chromosomes contain proteins but no DNA base sequence.
questions.explanation: Humans have 22 pairs of autosomes and one pair of sex chromosomes.

questions.prompt: A karyotype shows sex chromosomes labelled \(XX\). What can be inferred from that pair?
choices: The sex chromosome pair contains two X chromosomes.
All 23 chromosome pairs are X-shaped sex chromosomes.
The autosomes must be missing from the karyotype.
The cell has no homologous chromosome pairs.
questions.explanation: XX describes the sex chromosome pair; it does not describe all chromosome pairs.

questions.prompt: Which misconception should be rejected?
choices: Autosomes and sex chromosomes can both be part of the same human chromosome set.
The X and Y chromosomes are counted as the sex chromosome pair.
Only sex chromosomes carry genes, while autosomes do not.
The remaining 22 pairs in a human karyotype are autosomes.
questions.explanation: Autosomes also carry many genes; sex chromosomes are only one pair in the full chromosome set.

questions.prompt: What makes two chromosomes homologous?
choices: They are sister chromatids made by copying one chromosome during replication.
They have the same length only; gene locations do not matter.
They are made from RNA instead of DNA.
They have the same sequence of gene locations, though they may carry different alleles.
questions.explanation: Homologous chromosomes carry genes for the same traits at corresponding locations but are not necessarily identical.

questions.prompt: When matching homologous chromosomes in a karyotype, which feature would be useful?
choices: Similar length, centromere location, and banding pattern.
Exactly the same allele at every gene location.
Whether both chromosomes were inherited from the same parent.
The amino acid sequence of a protein made somewhere else in the cell.
questions.explanation: Homologous chromosomes can be compared using physical features such as length, centromere position, and banding pattern.

questions.prompt: Why are homologous chromosomes not always identical?
choices: One chromosome in each homologous pair contains genes, while the other contains no DNA.
Homologous chromosomes must always have identical alleles to be paired.
They can carry different versions of the same genes.
Homologous chromosomes are only the X and Y chromosomes.
questions.explanation: A homologous pair can contain the same genes but different alleles.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes a gene from an allele?
choices: A gene is a whole chromosome pair; an allele is the nucleus that stores it.
A gene is a DNA section for a trait; an allele is a version of that gene.
A gene is a protein product; an allele is the RNA copy of that product.
Genes occur only on autosomes, while alleles occur only on sex chromosomes.
questions.explanation: A gene is a section of DNA connected to a trait, and alleles are different forms of the same gene.

questions.prompt: Two homologous chromosomes carry information for hair type at the same location, but one version is linked with straight hair and the other with curly hair. What are these versions called?
choices: Autosomes.
Karyotypes.
Chromatids.
Alleles.
questions.explanation: Different forms of the same gene are called alleles.

questions.prompt: Which misconception about alleles is best corrected?
choices: Alleles are not different genes for unrelated traits; they are different forms of the same gene.
Alleles are chromosome pairs arranged from longest to shortest.
Alleles are proteins that replace genes during cell division.
Alleles are sugar-phosphate backbones with no base sequence.
questions.explanation: Alleles refer to alternate forms of a gene at a corresponding gene location.

questions.prompt: What is a karyotype used to show?
choices: The RNA copy made from one active gene.
The exact base sequence of every chromosome.
A photographed and arranged set of homologous chromosome pairs.
The folded shape of one protein molecule.
questions.explanation: A karyotype displays chromosomes arranged in pairs so chromosome number and type can be examined.

questions.prompt: A student studies a human karyotype to identify whether the sex chromosomes are \(XX\) or \(XY\). Which part should they examine?
choices: The sex chromosome pair placed apart from the numbered autosomes.
Only the longest autosome pair, because it determines genetic sex.
The cytoplasm around the photographed chromosomes.
The sequence of bases in a copied RNA strand.
questions.explanation: Human karyotypes arrange autosomes in numbered pairs and label the sex chromosomes separately.

questions.prompt: Which statement about karyotypes is most accurate?
choices: They show all alleles in base-by-base detail.
They help examine chromosome number, pairs, and sex chromosomes, but they do not show every base sequence.
They show proteins after DNA has been translated.
They show chromosomes only if the cell has no autosomes.
questions.explanation: Karyotypes are useful for viewing chromosome organization, not for reading every nucleotide.

questions.prompt: What is DNA replication?
choices: The process of translating an RNA sequence into an amino acid chain.
The process of condensing chromatin into visible chromosomes.
The process of pairing homologous chromosomes in a karyotype.
The process of making an exact copy of a DNA molecule.
questions.explanation: Replication copies DNA so genetic information can be passed to new cells.

questions.prompt: Why must a cell replicate its DNA before cell division?
choices: So each chromosome can lose one strand before the cell divides.
So proteins can replace chromosomes in the daughter cells.
So each new cell can receive a complete set of genetic information.
So the cell can copy only the genes it used most recently.
questions.explanation: Before division, DNA must be copied so daughter cells can inherit genetic information.

questions.prompt: Which feature of DNA helps replication produce matching new strands?
choices: Random pairing of bases whenever the molecule unzips.
Complementary base pairing.
The absence of nitrogenous bases from DNA.
Genes being located only on one chromosome in each pair.
questions.explanation: Base-pairing rules allow each original strand to guide formation of a complementary new strand.

questions.prompt: What does it mean for a cell to copy its genome before division?
choices: The cell copies the genes most active in that cell type first.
The cell converts each chromosome into RNA before division.
The cell makes a copy of its complete DNA set, not just one small section.
The cell copies the sex chromosomes separately from the autosomes.
questions.explanation: Genome copying means the cell replicates its complete DNA set so genetic information can be inherited accurately.

questions.prompt: Why is copying the whole genome important before cell division?
choices: Each new cell needs a complete set of genetic instructions.
The larger daughter cell needs extra chromosomes after division.
DNA is used up during interphase, so daughter cells need brand-new genes.
Copying the genome removes alleles that are not expressed.
questions.explanation: Replication prepares genetic information so daughter cells can receive the DNA they need.

questions.prompt: Which statement best corrects a misconception about genome copying?
choices: Genome copying happens after daughter cells have already separated.
Genome copying means copying the chromosome pair that controls the next cell activity.
A cell should repeatedly copy its genome whenever it needs a protein.
A cell copies its DNA once during the cell cycle, not randomly again and again.
questions.explanation: The textbook emphasizes that DNA replication happens once for the genome during the cell cycle.

kp-22.title: KP3: Template strands and complementary strands

questions.prompt: During DNA replication, what role does an original strand play?
choices: It is copied into a strand with the exact same bases across from it.
It acts as a template for building a complementary new strand.
It is discarded before any new DNA molecule can form.
It becomes the amino acid chain of a protein.
questions.explanation: An original DNA strand can guide formation of a matching complementary strand.

questions.prompt: If an original DNA strand begins \(TACG\), which new complementary sequence should form across from it?
choices: \(TACG\)
\(TGCA\)
\(ATGC\)
\(CGTA\)
questions.explanation: Use base-pairing rules: T pairs with A, A with T, C with G, and G with C.

questions.prompt: Which statement describes the two DNA molecules formed by replication?
choices: Each new DNA molecule has one original strand and one newly made complementary strand.
One DNA molecule keeps both original strands, while the second is built from two new strands.
Both resulting molecules are RNA until proteins convert them back into DNA.
Both resulting molecules contain newly made strands paired together first.
questions.explanation: The copied DNA molecules each include an original template strand paired with a new strand.

kp-23.title: KP4: DNA to RNA to protein

questions.prompt: Which pathway best summarizes how genetic information is used to make a protein?
choices: RNA information is stored as DNA after the protein sequence is checked.
A protein is copied into RNA, and RNA then edits the DNA sequence.
DNA changes directly into amino acids without an RNA copy.
DNA information is copied into RNA, and RNA helps direct protein assembly.
questions.explanation: The concept links a gene in DNA to an RNA copy, then to the assembly of amino acids in a protein.

questions.prompt: A cell needs a protein for a cellular activity. What is the most likely first genetic step?
choices: The cell translates an amino acid chain before any gene is accessed.
The cell accesses the DNA information in a gene and makes an RNA copy.
The cell condenses its chromosomes so proteins can assemble without a template.
The cell copies the shortest chromosome because it is fastest to use.
questions.explanation: A gene's DNA sequence can be copied into RNA as part of the process leading to protein production.

questions.prompt: Which misconception about RNA is best corrected?
choices: RNA permanently replaces DNA as the inherited genome in body cells.
RNA is just another name for a homologous chromosome pair.
RNA is involved in carrying genetic information from DNA toward protein production.
RNA is mainly used to make a second complete genome before division.
questions.explanation: RNA is part of the information pathway between DNA and proteins.

kp-24.title: KP5: Base sequence and protein sequence

questions.prompt: Why can a change in a DNA base sequence affect a protein?
choices: DNA base order helps determine RNA order, which helps determine amino acid order.
Chromosome length matters more than the exact base order in a gene.
The sugar-phosphate backbone carries most of the amino acid instructions.
A protein sequence changes when the organism's surroundings change.
questions.explanation: The DNA sequence influences the RNA sequence, and RNA helps determine the amino acid sequence of a protein.

questions.prompt: Two versions of a gene differ by a few bases. What is a reasonable possible result?
choices: They should produce the same RNA if the two versions have similar length.
They can change a karyotype photo more directly than a molecule in the cell.
They change the chromosome number before they can affect a protein.
They may lead to different amino acid sequences in a protein.
questions.explanation: Because sequence matters, differences in DNA can sometimes change the resulting protein.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains the importance of sequence rather than just quantity?
choices: Any DNA segment with the same number of bases must code for the same protein.
The order of bases carries information, much like the order of letters changes a word.
A protein sequence depends mainly on whether DNA is condensed into chromatin.
Amino acids assemble in a random order whenever RNA is present.
questions.explanation: Genetic information depends on sequence; order can affect the RNA and protein produced.

questions.prompt: Which set lists the three levels of biodiversity emphasized in this section?
choices: Animal diversity, plant diversity, and bacteria diversity.
Chromosome diversity, RNA diversity, and protein diversity.
Genetic diversity, species diversity, and ecosystem diversity.
Weather diversity, soil diversity, and mineral diversity.
questions.explanation: Biodiversity includes variation at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels.

questions.prompt: A wetland contains many species, and nearby forests and grasslands form different habitats. Which biodiversity level is most about the variety of habitats and ecosystems?
choices: Ecosystem diversity.
Species diversity, because habitats are counted like organisms.
Genetic diversity, because ecosystems are compared by their genes.
Allele diversity, because abiotic factors are separate from biodiversity.
questions.explanation: Ecosystem diversity concerns the variety of ecosystems in the biosphere.

questions.prompt: Which statement corrects a common oversimplification?
choices: Biodiversity is mostly another word for species diversity.
Biodiversity includes ecosystems but not inherited variation.
Biodiversity includes genes but not the abundance of species.
Biodiversity means more than just counting how many species live in an area.
questions.explanation: Species diversity is important, but biodiversity also includes genetic and ecosystem diversity.

questions.prompt: What does genetic diversity describe?
choices: The number of different ecosystems in the biosphere.
Variation in inherited traits within a species or population.
The abundance of different species in one wetland.
The non-living conditions that shape a habitat.
questions.explanation: Genetic diversity refers to inherited variation among individuals.

questions.prompt: Which example best represents genetic diversity within one species?
choices: Different inherited colour patterns among individuals in one population.
A lake, a desert, and a forest existing in different regions.
Many species living together in one freshwater wetland.
Different abiotic conditions such as light, soil nutrients, and climate.
questions.explanation: Inherited trait differences among members of a species are evidence of genetic diversity.

questions.prompt: Which misconception about genetic diversity should be rejected?
choices: Genetic diversity can be seen as inherited variation among individuals.
Different alleles can contribute to different traits in a population.
Members of one species should have the same alleles if they interbreed.
Variation in genes can help explain differences within a species.
questions.explanation: Members of the same species can vary because they may carry different alleles.

questions.prompt: What is a gene pool?
choices: The alleles found in one selected individual from a population.
The total number of ecosystems where a species can live.
A list of the abiotic factors that affect one habitat.
The full set of gene versions in a population.
questions.explanation: A gene pool includes the alleles present across a population.

questions.prompt: Why can a species have more genetic diversity than one population of that species?
choices: Each population usually contains the same allele combinations.
Different populations can contain different combinations of alleles.
A species has genetic diversity when populations have identical gene pools.
Separate populations lose their gene pools when compared.
questions.explanation: Separate populations may have different gene pools, so the species as a whole can include more variation.

questions.prompt: A population of insects contains several alleles for body colour. What does this add to?
choices: The population's gene pool.
The number of ecosystems in the region.
The abiotic conditions of the insects' habitat.
The species definition, but not genetic diversity.
questions.explanation: The alleles present in members of a population are part of that population's gene pool.

questions.prompt: In this section, what is a species?
choices: Organisms that live in the same ecosystem, even if they do not reproduce together.
A population with very little visible genetic variation.
A group of organisms that can interbreed in nature and produce fertile offspring.
A group of organisms that share one habitat but do not produce fertile offspring.
questions.explanation: The textbook definition focuses on interbreeding in nature and producing fertile offspring.

questions.prompt: Which observation is most directly about species diversity?
choices: Two birds of one species have different inherited feather patterns.
A single gene has several alleles in one population.
A region contains forests, wetlands, and grasslands.
A park has many different species, and some are very abundant.
questions.explanation: Species diversity concerns the variety and abundance of species in an area.

questions.prompt: Which statement avoids confusing species diversity with genetic diversity?
choices: Species diversity counts allele differences inside one gene pool.
Species diversity compares kinds and abundance of species, while genetic diversity compares inherited variation within species or populations.
Genetic diversity is the variety of ecosystems in the biosphere.
Species diversity and genetic diversity usually measure the same pattern.
questions.explanation: The two levels are connected but describe different scales of biodiversity.

questions.prompt: What does ecosystem diversity describe?
choices: The variety of ecosystems in the biosphere.
The variety of alleles for one trait within a population.
The number and abundance of species in one sample plot.
The RNA sequences copied from a gene.
questions.explanation: Ecosystem diversity is the largest biodiversity scale described here.

questions.prompt: Which pair includes one biotic factor and one abiotic factor that can shape an ecosystem?
choices: Two alleles of the same gene.
Two homologous chromosomes in one cell.
A plant species and soil nutrients.
Two RNA copies made from the same gene.
questions.explanation: Biotic factors are living organisms; abiotic factors include non-living conditions such as soil nutrients.

questions.prompt: Why can ecosystem diversity be high across Earth's surface?
choices: Species in different ecosystems usually have the same gene pools.
Each ecosystem is classified mostly by abiotic factors rather than living organisms.
Chromosome number changes from place to place more than communities do.
Different places have different living communities and non-living conditions.
questions.explanation: Variation in organisms and abiotic conditions helps create many different ecosystems.

questions.prompt: How can genetic variation support biodiversity?
choices: Genetic variation matters mainly inside one cell and has little effect on traits.
Differences in genes can produce trait variation that helps populations and species differ.
Genetic variation makes organisms more similar within a species.
Genes affect traits, while abiotic factors determine biodiversity by themselves.
questions.explanation: Variation in genes can lead to inherited trait differences, which contribute to biodiversity.

questions.prompt: A population contains inherited differences that help some individuals survive local conditions. Which larger idea does this support?
choices: Genetic differences can contribute to the persistence and diversity of life forms.
A population with inherited variation has too many alleles to describe as a gene pool.
Species diversity is unrelated to changes in genetic variation.
Ecosystem diversity matters more than traits within a species.
questions.explanation: Genetic variation can affect traits, survival, and eventually patterns of biodiversity.

questions.prompt: Which statement best connects the three biodiversity levels?
choices: Ecosystem diversity is smaller in scale than the alleles in one gene.
Species diversity increases when individuals within each species have identical genes.
Genetic variation within populations can influence species differences and the communities found in ecosystems.
Genetic, species, and ecosystem diversity are completely unrelated categories.
questions.explanation: The levels of biodiversity are related: genetic variation helps shape species and ecosystems over time.

questions.prompt: What was a major goal of the Human Genome Project?
choices: To identify proteins in selected human blood samples.
To test whether most traits are controlled by one chromosome.
To determine the complete sequence of the human genome and identify human genes.
To replace chromosome studies with karyotypes alone.
questions.explanation: The project focused on reading the human genome sequence and identifying genes.

questions.prompt: Why can genome sequencing help scientists understand human genetic information?
choices: It shows the order of DNA bases that can be studied for gene locations and functions.
It shows chromosome size better than base order.
It changes a person's inherited traits as soon as the sequence is read.
It proves that DNA outside genes has no possible function.
questions.explanation: A genome sequence provides DNA base-order data that researchers can analyze.

questions.prompt: Which statement avoids a common misconception about the Human Genome Project?
choices: The project answered most questions about how each human trait works.
The project proved that non-human genomes are less useful for human research.
The project showed that genes are unrelated to DNA sequence.
Completing the sequence was a major step, but interpreting the genome continues.
questions.explanation: Genome sequencing produced important data, but understanding all of that information is ongoing work.

questions.prompt: Which finding best matches the human genome information in this section?
choices: The human genome has about 21 base pairs and about 3 billion genes.
The human genome has about \(3 \times 10^9\) base pairs and about 21 000 genes.
The human genome is mostly protein-coding genes with little other DNA.
The human genome is mainly a list of proteins rather than DNA bases.
questions.explanation: The section connects the human genome with about three billion base pairs and about 21 000 genes.

questions.prompt: If only about 1.5% of human DNA directly codes for genes, about what percent does not directly code for genes?
choices: About 3.0%.
About 21%.
About 98.5%.
About 1.5%.
questions.explanation: Subtract 1.5% from 100%: about 98.5% is not directly gene-coding DNA.

questions.prompt: Which statement best corrects a misconception about non-coding DNA?
choices: Some DNA outside genes can still be important even if it does not directly code for proteins.
DNA outside genes is not part of chromosomes.
Protein-coding DNA is the main DNA included in a genome sequence.
The existence of non-coding DNA proves genes are not made of DNA.
questions.explanation: The textbook notes that parts of the DNA outside genes are important, even though much remains poorly understood.

questions.prompt: Why does genome research raise ethical questions?
choices: Genetic information is usually separate from families or communities.
Genetic information becomes more useful when it is made public.
Ethics applies mainly to plant breeding rather than human DNA data.
Genetic information can be personal and may affect privacy, ownership, and use of data.
questions.explanation: Genetic data can be sensitive, so its ownership, control, access, and use need careful consideration.

questions.prompt: A research group wants to study DNA data from a small isolated community. What concern is most relevant?
choices: Whether chromosome number makes consent unnecessary.
How consent, privacy, community impact, and control of information will be handled.
Whether communities should have similar gene pools before research begins.
Whether DNA data should be treated as non-personal once it is sequenced.
questions.explanation: Genetic research can affect individuals and communities, so consent and careful regulation matter.

questions.prompt: Which policy would best fit responsible use of genetic information?
choices: Allowing any researcher to reuse genetic data for any purpose.
Treating consent as unnecessary once DNA has been collected.
Clear rules for consent, privacy, access, and how data may be used.
Avoiding discussion of who owns or controls genetic information.
questions.explanation: Responsible genetics work requires protection and thoughtful regulation of sensitive information.

questions.prompt: How are hybrid crop varieties usually produced?
choices: By crossing two plant lines to emphasize selected traits.
By preserving older seeds in a seed bank without crossing plants.
By reducing inherited variation so crop plants become easier to sort.
By choosing plants mainly because they do not reproduce by pollination.
questions.explanation: Hybrids are produced by crossing plant lines to combine or enhance chosen traits.

questions.prompt: Which trait might breeders try to enhance in a hybrid crop?
choices: The ability to grow without inherited traits.
The absence of chromosomes in harvested seeds.
A guarantee that offspring seeds will keep the same selected traits.
Longer shelf life.
questions.explanation: Hybrid breeding may focus on traits such as size, appearance, or storage life.

questions.prompt: Which statement fairly compares a possible benefit and limitation of hybrids?
choices: They preserve regional heritage gene pools better when they have strong market traits.
They may have selected market traits, but some hybrid crops may not produce fertile seeds for replanting.
They are defined as non-patented plants that are at least 50 years old.
They are useful because they contain fewer inherited trait differences.
questions.explanation: Hybrids can be bred for useful traits, but they may create dependence on buying new seed.

questions.prompt: Which description best matches native and heritage crop varieties in this section?
choices: New hybrid lines selected mainly for longer shelf life.
Plants bred for shelf life that may need new seed purchases each year.
Naturally pollinated, non-patented plants that can produce fertile seeds.
Plants defined by having no regional history or adaptations.
questions.explanation: The section contrasts native and heritage varieties with many commercial hybrids.

questions.prompt: Why can local heritage varieties be useful in a region?
choices: They may be adapted to local conditions and show resistance to pests or disease.
They are useful when local adaptation does not affect crop survival.
They replace regional alleles with alleles from commercial hybrids.
They may need more irrigation if they are not matched to local conditions.
questions.explanation: Local adaptation can help crops tolerate regional environmental pressures.

questions.prompt: Which statement connects heritage varieties to genetic diversity?
choices: Saving heritage varieties makes crop populations more uniform each generation.
Heritage crops affect taste more than the crop gene pool.
Hybrid crops contain inherited variation more reliably than local varieties.
Maintaining more plant varieties can keep more alleles in the gene pool.
questions.explanation: Preserving more varieties can help maintain genetic diversity within crops.

kp-36.title: KP3: Seed libraries and seed banks

questions.prompt: What is one main difference between a seed library and a seed bank?
choices: A seed library preserves reference labels, while a seed bank lends planting guides.
A seed library distributes seeds and information, while a seed bank preserves seeds for future use.
A seed library is mainly for hybrid crops, while a seed bank is mainly for animals.
A seed library removes local varieties, while a seed bank prevents storage.
questions.explanation: Seed libraries emphasize sharing seeds and information; seed banks emphasize long-term preservation.

questions.prompt: Why might seeds be dried and kept cold in a seed bank?
choices: To make stored seeds develop the same traits before planting.
To convert heritage varieties into patented hybrids.
To help preserve them for long periods in case seed reserves are needed later.
To stop the seeds from being useful after long storage.
questions.explanation: Cold, dry storage can help preserve seeds for future recovery or use.

questions.prompt: Which scenario best shows seed preservation supporting biodiversity?
choices: A community stores and shares several local crop varieties so their genetic diversity is not lost.
A farm grows one uniform variety and stores the package labels.
A seed bank stores labels but no seeds or living material.
A crop program focuses on one high-yield variety and sets aside local plants.
questions.explanation: Seed sharing and banking can help keep diverse plant varieties available.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best describes how scientists prepare chromosomes for a karyotype?
choices: Collect cells, sequence base regions, then arrange genes by function.
Collect chromosomes after meiosis, then sort by allele dominance.
Collect RNA, translate it, and photograph the protein products.
Collect cells, stop them during mitosis, stain chromosomes, then photograph them.
questions.explanation: Karyotype preparation involves obtaining cells and making chromosomes visible for imaging.

questions.prompt: Why are cells stopped during mitosis when preparing a karyotype?
choices: The DNA sequence becomes easier to read base by base during mitosis.
Chromosomes are condensed and easier to see during mitosis.
Mitosis separates homologous pairs so their alleles can be compared.
Chromosomes are easiest to photograph during mitosis because they are fully stretched out.
questions.explanation: Condensed chromosomes are more visible and can be photographed for analysis.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of staining chromosomes in karyotype preparation?
choices: To change chromosome number before the photograph is taken.
To turn autosomes into sex chromosomes.
To make chromosome patterns easier to see under a microscope.
To make homologous chromosomes identical in size.
questions.explanation: Staining improves visibility and can reveal patterns useful for matching chromosomes.

questions.prompt: How are chromosomes commonly arranged in a human karyotype?
choices: In homologous pairs, ordered from largest to smallest, with sex chromosomes placed last.
By whether each chromosome carries a dominant allele.
As one long DNA molecule without pairs.
By RNA sequence after proteins are made.
questions.explanation: Karyotypes organize chromosomes by pairing and size, with autosomes numbered and sex chromosomes identified.

questions.prompt: Which features help match homologous chromosomes in a karyotype?
choices: The colour of the paper used in the classroom model.
Whether both chromosomes carry identical alleles.
The order in which chromosomes happened to be photographed.
Similar size, shape, and banding pattern.
questions.explanation: Chromosome size, shape, and banding patterns help identify pairs.

questions.prompt: A karyotype has 22 numbered autosome pairs and a pair labelled \(XY\). What does \(XY\) identify?
choices: A missing autosome pair.
The sex chromosome pair.
A pair of chromosomes with no genes.
The complete sequence of chromosome 22.
questions.explanation: X and Y are sex chromosomes; the other numbered pairs are autosomes.

questions.prompt: What type of evidence can a karyotype reveal?
choices: The detailed base sequence of alleles in the genome.
The shelf life of a hybrid crop variety.
An abnormal chromosome number or a large chromosome structure change.
The amino acid sequence of proteins in the cell.
questions.explanation: Karyotypes are useful for spotting chromosome number and large structural abnormalities.

questions.prompt: A karyotype shows three copies of chromosome 21. Which interpretation fits this evidence?
choices: Trisomy 21.
A normal homologous pair of chromosome 21.
A single-base substitution in one allele.
A missing sex chromosome with no autosome abnormality.
questions.explanation: Trisomy means three copies of a chromosome; three chromosome 21s indicate trisomy 21.

questions.prompt: Why can a karyotype miss some genetic disorders?
choices: Karyotypes show RNA molecules more clearly than chromosomes.
Single genes are usually visible as separate bands on a chromosome.
Karyotypes are mainly used for plant seed storage.
Some disorders involve changes in a single gene that are too small to see in a chromosome photograph.
questions.explanation: A karyotype shows chromosome-scale features, not every nucleotide-level mutation.

questions.prompt: What does genetics study?
choices: Traits learned after birth rather than inherited traits.
Heredity, or how traits pass from parents to offspring.
How seeds are dried and stored for later planting.
Chromosome photographs used in karyotypes.
questions.explanation: Genetics is the field of biology that studies heredity.

questions.prompt: Which example best fits heredity?
choices: A plant grows taller because it was watered more than another plant.
A metal spoon warms when placed in hot soup.
An offspring inherits alleles that influence a trait from its parents.
A seed bank keeps seeds dry and cold for preservation.
questions.explanation: Heredity involves inherited genetic information influencing traits across generations.

questions.prompt: Which statement corrects a misconception about inherited traits?
choices: Inherited traits are passed through genetic information, although the environment can also affect how traits appear.
Heredity means offspring must be identical to their parents.
Traits are learned from the environment before genes can affect them.
Genetics applies to crops and animals but not humans.
questions.explanation: Genetics explains inherited information from parents to offspring, while real traits can also be shaped by context.

questions.prompt: Which description best matches a trait in genetics?
choices: The allele combination written with letters such as \(Bb\).
A non-living factor such as soil nutrients or rainfall.
An inherited characteristic, such as flower colour or hair colour.
A chart used to arrange photographed chromosomes.
questions.explanation: A trait is an inherited characteristic connected to genetic information.

questions.prompt: Which example is most clearly an observable inherited trait?
choices: A pea plant's flower colour.
The temperature of the room where the plant grows.
The brand of microscope used to view the cells.
The page number where the trait is described.
questions.explanation: Flower colour can be an inherited characteristic and can be observed as a phenotype.

questions.prompt: Which statement corrects a misconception about traits?
choices: Traits can be influenced by inherited alleles, but the environment may also affect how some traits appear.
Traits are identical to genotype and separate from phenotype.
Traits are studied mainly in plants rather than animals.
Traits are abiotic conditions that shape an ecosystem.
questions.explanation: Genetics studies inherited traits, while real organisms may also be affected by environmental conditions.

questions.prompt: Why were Mendel's controlled pea-plant crosses useful for studying inheritance?
choices: He allowed random pollination and focused on unusual offspring.
He could choose which plants produced offspring and track the traits carefully.
He studied many unrelated traits at once without recording parent plants.
He removed inherited traits before breeding plants.
questions.explanation: Controlled crosses and careful records helped Mendel identify patterns in inherited traits.

questions.prompt: What is cross-pollination in the pea-plant context?
choices: A flower fertilizes itself using gametes from the same flower.
A chromosome is photographed and arranged by size.
A male gamete from one plant combines with a female gamete from a different plant.
A seed is dried and stored at low temperature.
questions.explanation: Cross-pollination involves gametes from different plants, which allowed Mendel to control parent traits.

questions.prompt: Which practice made Mendel's work more scientific?
choices: Changing the question after the plants grew.
Counting the results that matched his prediction first.
Avoiding repeated crosses so no pattern could be checked.
Keeping careful records and analyzing offspring trait patterns.
questions.explanation: Methodical records and repeated observations made the inheritance patterns clearer.

questions.prompt: What is a true-breeding plant?
choices: A plant whose offspring show both forms of a trait in equal numbers.
A plant that consistently produces offspring with one form of a trait when self-fertilized.
A plant that passes random alleles unrelated to the studied trait.
A plant with no homologous chromosomes.
questions.explanation: True-breeding plants produce predictable offspring for the trait being studied.

questions.prompt: In Mendel-style notation, what does \(F_1\) usually refer to?
choices: The parent generation before a cross.
The generation produced after \(F_1\) plants self-fertilize.
The first generation of offspring from a cross.
The ratio of dominant to recessive alleles.
questions.explanation: F1 means the first filial generation, or first offspring generation.

questions.prompt: A recessive trait seems to disappear in \(F_1\) offspring but reappears in some \(F_2\) offspring. What does this suggest?
choices: The allele was removed from the \(F_1\) generation.
The trait is not inherited when it is hidden in \(F_1\).
The \(F_2\) generation has no genetic material.
The allele can be carried without being expressed in the heterozygous phenotype.
questions.explanation: A recessive allele can be hidden by a dominant allele and then reappear when two recessive alleles combine.

questions.prompt: What does the law of segregation state?
choices: Alleles for a trait separate during gamete formation, so each gamete carries one allele.
Both alleles for a trait enter the same gamete together.
Recessive alleles separate during gamete formation, while dominant alleles stay paired.
Alleles disappear before fertilization and are remade later.
questions.explanation: During meiosis, allele pairs separate so gametes carry one allele for each trait.

questions.prompt: An organism has genotype \(Bb\) for a trait. Which alleles can its gametes carry for that trait?
choices: \(BB\) as the usual gamete form.
\(Bb\) in most gametes.
No allele for the trait.
questions.explanation: The two alleles separate, so each gamete receives either B or b.

questions.prompt: How does fertilization relate to segregation?
choices: Gametes prevent chromosomes from pairing in the offspring.
Fertilization removes recessive alleles that were hidden in the parents.
Gametes combine and restore paired alleles in the offspring.
Fertilization happens before gametes form.
questions.explanation: Segregation separates alleles into gametes; fertilization brings one allele from each parent together.

questions.prompt: If \(B\) is dominant for purple flowers and \(b\) is recessive for white flowers, which genotype would show white flowers?
choices: \(B\) written without the second allele.
questions.explanation: A recessive phenotype appears only when both alleles are recessive.

questions.prompt: Which genotype would express the dominant trait if \(B\) is dominant?
choices: A genotype with two recessive alleles.
A gamete with \(b\), before fertilization.
questions.explanation: One dominant allele is enough for the dominant trait to be expressed.

questions.prompt: Which allele-symbol convention is commonly used?
choices: Lowercase for dominant and uppercase for recessive.
Numbers instead of letters for the two alleles.
Uppercase for the dominant allele and lowercase for the recessive allele.
Different unrelated letters for two alleles of the same gene.
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is usually shown with an uppercase letter and the recessive version with the matching lowercase letter.

kp-46.title: KP7: Genotype, phenotype, homozygous, and heterozygous

questions.prompt: What is the difference between genotype and phenotype?
choices: Genotype is the visible trait; phenotype is the chromosome photograph.
Genotype describes environment, while phenotype describes seed storage.
There is no difference between the two terms.
Genotype is the allele combination; phenotype is the expressed trait.
questions.explanation: Genotype describes alleles, while phenotype describes the trait that is expressed or observed.

questions.prompt: Which genotype is heterozygous?
choices: \(R\) written by itself.
questions.explanation: Heterozygous means two different alleles for a trait.

questions.prompt: Two pea plants both have purple flowers, but one is \(BB\) and the other is \(Bb\). What is the key lesson?
choices: A visible trait usually reveals the exact genotype.
Heterozygous organisms usually show a recessive trait.
The same phenotype can come from different genotypes.
Dominant alleles are expressed mainly in homozygous genotypes.
questions.explanation: A dominant phenotype can occur in both homozygous dominant and heterozygous organisms.

questions.prompt: What is a genetic cross?
choices: A chart of chromosome photographs arranged by size.
A method for storing heritage seeds.
A mutation that removes a chromosome from one parent.
Deliberate breeding between two parents to study offspring inheritance.
questions.explanation: A genetic cross studies how genetic material from two parents can combine in offspring.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to identify parent genotypes in an inheritance problem?
choices: Parent genotypes tell you the exact number of ecosystems nearby.
Parent genotypes determine which alleles can appear in their gametes.
Parent genotypes are unrelated to offspring possibilities.
Parent genotypes replace the need for fertilization.
questions.explanation: The alleles parents can pass on come from their genotypes.

questions.prompt: A cross is written \(Bb \times bb\). What does this notation show?
choices: The exact phenotype of each offspring after birth.
The number of chromosomes in a karyotype.
The genotypes of the two parents being crossed.
The total number of genes in the human genome.
questions.explanation: Cross notation lists the parent genotypes used to predict possible offspring genotypes.

questions.prompt: What does a monohybrid cross study?
choices: Inheritance of one trait or one gene at a time.
Several inherited and environmental traits in an organism at once.
Chromosome number in a karyotype.
The preservation of seeds in a cold bank.
questions.explanation: A monohybrid cross focuses on one trait being inherited.

questions.prompt: Which situation is best modeled as a monohybrid cross?
choices: Comparing species and ecosystems in one wetland.
Counting base pairs across the human genome.
Predicting offspring coat colour using one gene with two alleles.
Preparing a karyotype from stained chromosomes.
questions.explanation: A single-gene trait with two alleles is a typical monohybrid-cross situation.

questions.prompt: Which statement avoids a common mistake?
choices: A monohybrid cross studies several traits at once.
A monohybrid cross is too simple to use a Punnett square.
A monohybrid cross requires parents with identical genotypes.
A monohybrid cross does not track the whole organism; it focuses on one trait.
questions.explanation: Mono- means one, so the cross focuses on one inherited trait.

questions.prompt: What does a Punnett square show?
choices: The number of bases in a gene.
Possible offspring genotypes based on alleles in parent gametes.
The location of ecosystems in the biosphere.
The visible traits of the parents without allele combinations.
questions.explanation: A Punnett square models possible allele combinations formed at fertilization.

questions.prompt: In a Punnett square, what do the alleles written along the top and side represent?
choices: Possible alleles carried by each parent's gametes.
The final phenotypes of adult offspring.
Chromosomes sorted from largest to smallest.
The percentage of DNA that does not code for genes.
questions.explanation: The top and side labels list possible gamete alleles from each parent.

questions.prompt: For a cross \(Rr \times rr\), which gamete alleles should be placed for the \(Rr\) parent?
choices: \(Rr\) and \(Rr\), because gametes carry both alleles.
\(rr\), because recessive alleles segregate first.
\(RR\), because the dominant allele is more likely to be passed on.
questions.explanation: A heterozygous parent can produce gametes carrying either R or r.

questions.prompt: What does a genotypic ratio compare?
choices: The visible traits while ignoring allele combinations.
The number of chromosomes in each karyotype pair.
The total number of ecosystems in an area.
The predicted allele combinations among offspring.
questions.explanation: A genotypic ratio compares predicted genotypes such as RR, Rr, and rr.

questions.prompt: A Punnett square has offspring genotypes \(RR\), \(Rr\), \(Rr\), and \(rr\). What is the genotypic ratio?
questions.explanation: Count the four boxes: one RR, two Rr, and one rr.

questions.prompt: If two of four Punnett-square boxes show genotype \(Bb\), what fraction of offspring is predicted to have genotype \(Bb\)?
questions.explanation: Two out of four boxes is 2/4, which simplifies to 1/2.

questions.prompt: What does a phenotypic ratio compare?
choices: The predicted allele combinations among offspring.
The alleles that one parent can place along the top of a Punnett square.
The predicted visible traits among offspring.
The birth order expected for offspring with each trait.
questions.explanation: A phenotypic ratio compares predicted expressed traits, not just allele combinations.

questions.prompt: A Punnett square predicts 3 black-haired offspring for every 1 red-haired offspring. How should this be interpreted?
choices: As a probability pattern expected over many offspring, not a guarantee for every group of four.
As a rule that a small group of four should match the ratio closely.
As proof that the recessive phenotype is less likely in early offspring.
As the genotype ratio, even if two different genotypes produce black hair.
questions.explanation: Punnett squares show probabilities; actual small groups can differ from predicted ratios.

questions.prompt: If two of four Punnett-square boxes predict a recessive phenotype, what probability is predicted for that phenotype?
choices: 100%, because the recessive allele is present somewhere in the cross.
questions.explanation: Two out of four boxes is 2/4, which simplifies to 1/2 or 50%.

questions.prompt: What happens in codominance?
choices: The heterozygote shows a blended phenotype halfway between both homozygotes.
Both alleles for a trait are fully expressed in a heterozygote.
The allele written first in the genotype is expressed more strongly.
The recessive allele is present but has little effect in the heterozygote.
questions.explanation: In codominance, both alleles are expressed rather than one hiding the other.

questions.prompt: Which example best suggests codominance?
choices: A red flower and white flower produce pink offspring.
A recessive trait appears when both alleles are recessive.
An animal shows both red hairs and white hairs in its coat.
A heterozygote looks like one homozygous parent.
questions.explanation: Seeing both traits together is a hallmark of codominance.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes codominance from simple dominant-recessive inheritance?
choices: In codominance, the heterozygote expresses both alleles instead of a single dominant effect.
In codominance, the heterozygote looks intermediate between both homozygotes.
In codominance, homozygous individuals show the trait more clearly than heterozygotes.
In codominance, the phenotype depends on chromosome number instead of alleles.
questions.explanation: Codominance changes the heterozygote pattern: both allele effects are visible.

questions.prompt: Why can codominant alleles be written with superscripts, such as \(H^R\) and \(H^W\)?
choices: They show which allele is dominant and which one must be hidden.
They show that the two alleles are carried on separate chromosome pairs.
They mean the genotype contains more than two alleles at once.
They are different alleles of the same gene, and both can be expressed.
questions.explanation: The capital gene letter identifies the gene, while superscripts identify the allele versions.

questions.prompt: What genotype would represent a roan heterozygote using red and white hair alleles?
choices: \(H^R h\), because white should be written as a lowercase recessive allele.
\(H^R H^R\), because red hair is one visible part of roan.
\(H^W H^W\), because white hair is one visible part of roan.
questions.explanation: A roan animal has one red allele and one white allele, so both superscripts appear.

questions.prompt: Which notation mistake should be avoided for codominant alleles?
choices: Using a shared capital gene letter with different superscripts.
Showing both alleles in a heterozygote.
Using unrelated gene letters for alleles that are versions of the same gene.
Writing a two-allele genotype for a diploid organism.
questions.explanation: Alleles of the same gene should be represented as versions of the same gene symbol.

questions.prompt: Why is a roan animal a useful example of codominance?
choices: Its coat can show both base-colour hairs and white hairs.
Its coat colour is a single blended shade with no separate hair colours.
Its coat colour can be explained by a recessive allele being hidden.
Its coat colour is determined mainly by whether the animal is male or female.
questions.explanation: A roan coat visibly expresses both allele effects.

questions.prompt: A red-haired cow \(H^R H^R\) is crossed with a white-haired bull \(H^W H^W\). What offspring genotype is expected if all offspring inherit one allele from each parent?
choices: \(HH\) with no superscripts because both alleles are dominant.
questions.explanation: Each offspring receives H^R from the red parent and H^W from the white parent.

questions.prompt: What would you expect if you looked closely at individual hairs on a roan coat?
choices: Each hair should look halfway between red and white.
Some hairs show one colour and some hairs show white.
The colour from the allele written first appears more strongly.
White hairs appear when the animal has no red-colour allele.
questions.explanation: Roan coat colour is not simply blended; both colour types can be present.

kp-55.title: KP4: Sickle cell anemia and hemoglobin

questions.prompt: What protein is affected in sickle cell anemia?
choices: A coat-colour protein that controls whether hair is red or white.
An enzyme that copies chromosomes before meiosis.
Hemoglobin, a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen.
A membrane protein found mainly in gametes.
questions.explanation: Sickle cell anemia is linked to a hemoglobin allele that affects red blood cells.

questions.prompt: How can the sickle cell allele affect body function?
choices: It can produce hemoglobin that changes red blood cell shape and reduces oxygen transport.
It changes the colour of red blood cells more than their shape or flow.
It affects oxygen transport mainly by changing blood plasma rather than red blood cell hemoglobin.
It is expressed mainly in white blood cells, so red blood cells are less involved.
questions.explanation: Sickled cells can pass poorly through small vessels and transport oxygen less effectively.

questions.prompt: Which connection best follows the textbook logic?
choices: Allele difference -> flower-colour blend -> red blood cell shape difference -> body effects.
Punnett square prediction -> chromosome number change -> hemoglobin disappears.
Genotype notation -> no protein difference -> no cell-shape difference.
Allele difference -> hemoglobin difference -> red blood cell shape difference -> body effects.
questions.explanation: The example connects a gene allele to protein structure, cell shape, and organism function.

questions.prompt: What genotype represents sickle cell trait in this section?
choices: \(Hb^S Hb^S\), because the sickle allele is present twice.
\(Hb^A Hb^S\).
\(Hb^A Hb^A\), because normal hemoglobin can still be present.
\(H^R H^W\), because both examples use superscripts.
questions.explanation: Sickle cell trait is the heterozygous genotype with one normal hemoglobin allele and one sickle cell allele.

questions.prompt: What red blood cells may a person with genotype \(Hb^A Hb^S\) have?
choices: Mostly sickled red blood cells, the same as \(Hb^S Hb^S\).
Normal red blood cells because \(Hb^A\) hides most effects of \(Hb^S\).
Both normal and some sickled red blood cells.
Red blood cells with no hemoglobin alleles expressed.
questions.explanation: The heterozygote can show effects of both hemoglobin alleles.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes sickle cell trait from sickle cell anemia?
choices: Sickle cell trait is heterozygous; sickle cell anemia occurs in homozygous \(Hb^S Hb^S\) individuals.
Sickle cell trait and sickle cell anemia use the same genotype but different environments.
Sickle cell trait occurs when both hemoglobin alleles make normal hemoglobin.
Sickle cell anemia is the heterozygous form with one normal allele.
questions.explanation: The genotype matters: heterozygotes have the trait, while Hb^S Hb^S individuals have sickle cell anemia.

questions.prompt: What is the advantage sometimes associated with sickle cell trait?
choices: Protection from inheriting the \(Hb^S\) allele from either parent.
Red blood cells that carry extra oxygen during malaria infection.
A guarantee that offspring are unlikely to have sickle cell anemia.
Resistance to malaria in environments where malaria is common.
questions.explanation: In some malaria-risk environments, the heterozygous sickle cell trait can provide a survival advantage.

questions.prompt: Why does environment matter when considering the sickle cell allele?
choices: Malaria changes \(Hb^A\) alleles into \(Hb^S\) alleles during a person's life.
An allele that has harmful effects in one genotype can persist if heterozygotes gain an advantage where malaria is common.
A survival advantage in one genotype means the allele is harmless in homozygotes.
The same allele has similar survival effects across environments.
questions.explanation: The effect of an allele on survival can depend on environmental pressures such as malaria.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains why the sickle cell allele can remain common in some populations?
choices: People with the allele usually have the same severe disease.
The allele is produced by mosquito exposure in each generation.
Heterozygotes may be more likely to survive malaria and pass on the allele.
The allele disappears when it is paired with \(Hb^A\).
questions.explanation: If heterozygotes have a survival advantage, the allele can be maintained in the gene pool.

questions.prompt: What happens in incomplete dominance?
choices: Neither allele completely hides the other in the heterozygote.
Both alleles are fully expressed as separate visible parts of the phenotype.
One allele becomes dominant in the F2 generation.
The heterozygote has the same phenotype as one homozygous parent.
questions.explanation: Incomplete dominance produces a heterozygous phenotype that is between the two homozygous phenotypes.

questions.prompt: Which example best fits incomplete dominance?
choices: A coat has separate red and white hairs visible together.
A recessive trait appears in \(bb\) offspring.
A heterozygote looks like the red-flowered homozygote.
Red flowers crossed with white flowers produce pink heterozygous flowers.
questions.explanation: Pink offspring from red and white parents are a classic intermediate phenotype example.

questions.prompt: How is incomplete dominance different from codominance?
choices: Incomplete dominance and codominance differ mainly in the symbols used.
Incomplete dominance has an intermediate heterozygote; codominance shows both allele effects together.
Incomplete dominance means both alleles disappear in the heterozygote.
Codominance usually follows a simple dominant-recessive ratio.
questions.explanation: The heterozygote pattern differs: intermediate in incomplete dominance, both expressed in codominance.

questions.prompt: What is an intermediate phenotype?
choices: A phenotype where both traits appear side by side without blending.
A phenotype that matches the homozygous dominant parent.
A heterozygous phenotype between the two homozygous phenotypes.
A phenotype that is predicted mainly by counting chromosomes.
questions.explanation: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygote is intermediate between the two homozygotes.

questions.prompt: In four o'clock plants, red \(C^R C^R\) crossed with white \(C^W C^W\) produces pink \(C^R C^W\). What does pink represent?
choices: The intermediate phenotype.
A phenotype where the white allele is completely hidden.
A codominant phenotype with separate red and white patches.
A phenotype caused by having an extra chromosome.
questions.explanation: Pink is between red and white, so it represents incomplete dominance.

questions.prompt: Which observation would make you suspect incomplete dominance rather than simple dominance?
choices: The heterozygote looks like the homozygous dominant individual.
Recessive homozygotes are the main group that show a trait.
The phenotype depends mainly on chromosome number.
The heterozygote looks different from both homozygotes and falls between them.
questions.explanation: An intermediate heterozygous phenotype is the clue for incomplete dominance.

questions.prompt: For incomplete dominance in four o'clock flower colour, what phenotypic ratio can result from \(C^R C^W \times C^R C^W\)?
choices: 3 red : 1 white, with no pink.
1 red : 2 pink : 1 white.
2 red : 1 pink : 1 white, because red is written first.
1 red : 1 white, with no heterozygotes.
questions.explanation: A heterozygote by heterozygote cross produces C^R C^R, two C^R C^W, and C^W C^W.

questions.prompt: Why can the phenotypic ratio match the genotypic ratio in incomplete dominance?
choices: The heterozygote is hidden by a dominant allele.
Recessive homozygotes are the main surviving genotype.
Each genotype has its own distinct phenotype.
The phenotype ratio is counted from gametes, not offspring.
questions.explanation: Because heterozygotes have a distinct intermediate phenotype, the three genotypes correspond to three phenotypes.

questions.prompt: A Punnett square for \(C^R C^W \times C^R C^W\) has two boxes with \(C^R C^W\). What fraction of offspring is predicted to be pink?
choices: 1, because offspring in this cross have at least one colour allele.
questions.explanation: Two of four boxes are heterozygous, so 2/4 = 1/2 are predicted to be pink.
